coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ions detection based on quantum dot-doped hydrogel optical fiber
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Minjuan
Guo, Jingjing
Yang, Changxi
coredata.dc:description: We develop a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CdTe quantum dots (QDs) doped hydrogel optical fiber for selective and on-site detection of Fe3+ ions in real time. The hydrogel fiber is made of a core-cladding structure with a step-index profile to confine light within the core. Two types of QDs, the green emissive thioglycolic acid capped quantum dots (gQDs) and red emissive N-Acetyl-l-cysteine capped QDs (rQDs), are synthesized and incorporated in the core of the hydrogel fiber. The rQDs can be selectively quenched by the Fe3+ ions that diffuse into the hydrogel matrix while the gQDs are immune to Fe3+ ions and exhibit stable fluorescence emission. By ratiometric detection of the fluorescence intensities of the two QDs, quantitative and selective detection of Fe3+ ions is achieved in a linear range of 0–3.5 μM with a detection limit of 14 nM. The cladded hydrogel fiber doped with QDs enables efficient laser excitation and fluorescence collection in water. Our results reveal that the ratiometric sensing based on multi-QDs doped fiber can be feasibly applied for real-time and on-site analysis of heavy metal ions in aqueous environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
CdTe quantum dots
Hydrogels
Optical fiber sensor
Iron ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber
Characteristics of the QDs and hydrogels
Fabrication of QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber with step-index profile
Selectivity and pH dependence of the QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, Fabrication steps of the fiber core doped with QDs. b, Cladding encapsulation. c, Optical setup for sensing of Fe3+ ions based on the ratiometric fiber sensor.

a, TEM micrograph of the TGA-capped gQDs. The inset shows size distribution of the TGA-capped gQDs. b, TEM micrograph of the NAC-capped rQDs. Inset, size distribution. c, Absorption spectra of the synthesized QDs. d, Emission spectra of QDs excited at 405 nm and the maximal excitation wavelength. e, Absorption spectra of PEGDA hydrogel, gQDs-PEGDA hydrogel and rQDs-PEGDA hydrogel. f, Emission spectra of QDs in polymerized hydrogel with 405 nm excitation.

a, Microscope images of hydrogel fibers with different diameters. 1.1/1.17 mm indicates a core diameter of 1.1 mm and cladding diameter of 1.17 mm. b, Photographs showing laser (405 nm) coupling to the QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber (core/cladding: 2.06/2.24 mm, length: 3 cm) through a pigtailed silica MMF. Left, laser off; right, laser on. c, The coupled silica fiber end cleaved at 12° to suppress the back reflection.

a, Quenching efficiency of the QDs-doped hydrogel fiber to various heavy metal ions. The concentration of each metal ions is 2.5 μM. b, Fluorescence spectra of QDs-doped hydrogel fiber in presence of coexisting metal ions. The mixed metal ions include Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, As2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Na+ and Fe3+. The concentration of each metal ions is 2.5 μM. c, pH dependence of the QDs-doped hydrogel fiber. Error bars in a and c are based on standard deviations (n = 3).

a, Fluorescence spectra at various concentration of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 to 3.5 μM. b, Linear plot of (F 0 − F)/F 0 versus concentration of Fe3+ ions. c, Time-lapse emission spectra in presence of Fe3+ ions (2.5 μM). d, Time responses of the fiber sensor to various concentration of Fe3+ ions. e, Time responses of the fibers at core/cladding diameters of 1.1/1.17 mm and 2.06/2.24 mm, respectively. The concentration of the Fe3+ ions was 2.5 μM. f, Linear plot of (F 0 − F)/F 0 versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions in tap water. Error bars in b, d, e and f are based on standard deviations (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ions detection based on quantum dot-doped hydrogel optical fiber

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Precision Measurement Technology and Instruments, Department of Precision Instruments, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Precision Measurement Technology and Instruments

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the current work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We develop a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CdTe quantum dots (QDs) doped hydrogel optical fiber for selective and on-site detection of Fe3+ ions in real time. The hydrogel fiber is made of a core-cladding structure with a step-index profile to confine light within the core. Two types of QDs, the green emissive thioglycolic acid capped quantum dots (gQDs) and red emissive N-Acetyl-l-cysteine capped QDs (rQDs), are synthesized and incorporated in the core of the hydrogel fiber. The rQDs can be selectively quenched by the Fe3+ ions that diffuse into the hydrogel matrix while the gQDs are immune to Fe3+ ions and exhibit stable fluorescence emission. By ratiometric detection of the fluorescence intensities of the two QDs, quantitative and selective detection of Fe3+ ions is achieved in a linear range of 0–3.5 μM with a detection limit of 14 nM. The cladded hydrogel fiber doped with QDs enables efficient laser excitation and fluorescence collection in water. Our results reveal that the ratiometric sensing based on multi-QDs doped fiber can be feasibly applied for real-time and on-site analysis of heavy metal ions in aqueous environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) element is ubiquitously distributed in biological systems and natural environments [1–3]. The human body, for example, contains approximately 4–5 g of Fe in various forms. Fe3+ ions, belong to one of the transition metal ions that are essentially involved in many metabolic processes including oxygen transport, DNA synthesis, and electron transport [1]. However, an excess of Fe3+ ions can lead to tissue or organ damage that is associated with diseases such as hepatitis, hemochromatosis, organ dysfunction, and neurodegenerative diseases [2–5]. Recently, it has been reported that Fe3+ is one of the most limiting factor for the primary productivity of phytoplankton [6]. To date, a variety of well-established methods for detection of Fe3+ ions have been reported, such as atomic adsorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8], electrochemical method [9], chemiluminescence method [10], spectrophotometry [11], and colorimetric methods [12], etc. Differ from some of these instrumental techniques requiring sophisticated instrumentation and expensive facilities, fluorescence spectrometry has attracted tremendous attentions, owing to its advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity and rapid response [13,14].

In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as highly promising nanomaterials for fluorescence-based sensing applications due to their high quantum yields, excellent photostability, and especially broad absorption bands that allow for a diverse selection of excitation wavelengths [15,16]. Apart from the intense interests of their applications in bioimaging and biodiagnosis [17], water-soluble QDs have been demonstrated to be a promising sensor probe for fluorescence-based sensing of heavy metal ions such as Fe3+ [18], Hg2+ [19], Pb2+ [20], Cu2+ [21], Cd2+ [22], etc. However, the main problem encountered by the QDs-based sensors is that the QDs probes typically need to be mixed with the target aqueous samples for the subsequent fluorescence measurements [23,24], which are not conducive to rapid on-site detection. One possible approach to overcome this problem is to immobilize QDs in a suitable matrix, which retains the properties of QDs as those in solutions and is permeable to the surrounding analytes [25–28]. Polymer hydrogels, widely used in biomedical applications [29–31], are one of the promising matrices for immobilization of the QDs, due to their porous nature. The porous matrix allows small molecules and ions penetrate into the hydrogels via diffusion, while QDs with size comparable or larger than pore size of the hydrogels can be entrapped in the polymer networks [32,33]. In previous research, we demonstrated a CDs-incorporated hydrogel waveguide for on-site detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous environments by monitoring changes of the fluorescence intensity [33]. However, detection of heavy metal ions directly from the fluorescence intensity suffered from many analyte-independent interferences, such as intensity fluctuation of the excitation source, changes of the environmental conditions and instrumental efficiency [23].

In this work, we present a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for real-time and on-site detection of Fe3+ ions based on QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber. To effectively guide the light within the core, a core-clad structure is demonstrated with a relative refractive index difference of 0.048 along the fiber, which allows efficient laser exaction and emission collection in water. The ratiometric configuration of the proposed sensor provides a built-in calibration to eliminate the analyte-independent interferences [24,28,34–36]. Two types of CdTe QDs, which possessed different emission bands, have been synthesized for ratiometric measurements. One of the QDs, coated with thioglycolic acid (TGA), exhibits green emission and is insensitive to metal ions, thus serving as a reference. The other QDs as the specific recognition element, coated with N-Acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC), are red emissive and show high selectivity of fluorescence quenching towards Fe3+ ions. To avoid mutual interferences, the green emissive QDs (gQDs) and red emissive QDs (rQDs) are doped in discrete sections of the hydrogel optical fiber. Light coupling to the hydrogel fiber is achieved through a pigtailed multimode silica fiber, which offers facile, portable and real-time on-site operation of the sensor device in aqueous environments.

Tellurium (reagent powder, 99.8%), Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O), thioglycolic acid (TGA), N-Acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), polyethyleneglycol diacrylate (PEGDA, 700 Da), 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-propiophenone, sodium alginate, CaCl2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were of analytical grade or above unless otherwise stated.

Standard metal ion solutions of Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, As2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Na+, and Fe3+ in concentration range of 0–3.5 μM were prepared in PBS (pH = 7.4) for selectivity measurements. For pH measurements, standard pH solution was used from 3.0 to 8.2. For pH below 2.0 or above 9.0, HCl (1 M) and NaOH (1 M) were used to adjust the aqueous solution to the required pH, where deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18 MΩ cm was used during solution preparations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of water-soluble CdTe QDs with different photoluminescent wavelengths were synthesized following previous reported procedures [37,38]. One was TGA-capped with green emission and the other was NAC-capped with red emission. Then, two hydrogel precursors respectively containing the TGA-capped gQDs and NAC-capped rQDs, were prepared by mixing with carefully degassed solution of 30% w/v PEGDA (700 Da), 1% w/v 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-propiophenone in DI water. Because of the different quantum yields, the concentration of TGA-capped gQDs was set at 2 × 10−4% w/v while NAC-capped rQDs was 1.2 × 10−4% w/v. The two precursors were then successively injected into a silicone tube mold (Cole-Parmer) by syringes to fabricate the fiber core and each precursor in the mold had a same length of about 1.5 cm (Fig. 1 a). For light coupling, a silica multimode fiber (MMF, core/cladding: 200/215 μm) was pigtailed to the hydrogel fiber by inserting a short section (∼5 mm) of the silica fiber end into the precursors and aligned to the center of the tube mold. UV lamp (365 nm, 5 mW cm−2) was employed for the photo-crosslinking of the precursors. After 5 min irradiation, polymerized hydrogel fiber pigtailed with silica optical fiber was pulled out of the tube mold. Dip-coating technique was employed to form the cladding. The fiber core was first dipped in a Na-alginate solution (1% w/v) and then immersed in of CaCl2 solution (0.1 M) for Ca2+ -induced crosslinking of alginate chains (Fig. 1b).

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the CdTe QDs were respectively obtained by a 120 kV TEM (Tecnai Spirit) and a FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker VERTEX70). The excitation and fluorescence spectra of the QDs were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI, F-4600). UV–vis spectrometer (HITACHI, U-3900) was used for recording the optical absorption spectra of QDs. Samples including QDs, PEGDA hydrogels and QDs-doped hydrogels were prepared in standard 1.5 mL (1 × 0.5 cm-wide) cuvettes (Fisher), and the optical attenuation of the hydrogels was measured using the UV–vis spectrometer.

Fig. 1c shows the optical setup for detection of the Fe3+ ions. A semiconductor 405 nm laser (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd.) was employed as a light source for simultaneous excitation of the TGA-capped gQDs and NAC-capped rQDs in the hydrogel fiber, via a 50:50 fiber coupler. The excitation laser was modulated by a 0.1 Hz rectangular pulse with 20% duty cycle. For sensing of Fe3+ ions, the hydrogel fiber was dipped in Fe3+ containing samples and the emission fluorescence was measured through the pigtailed fiber and guided to a spectrometer (Ocean optics, Maya 2000). A long-pass filter (Semrock) with a cut-off wavelength of 430 nm was used to remove the excitation laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the QDs and hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized QDs are monodisperse and homogeneous as seen from their TEM images (Fig. 2 a and b). The average diameters of the TGA-capped gQDs and NAC-capped rQDs are 1.94 nm and 3.42 nm, respectively (insets of Fig. 2a and b). FTIR spectra were taken to identify the functional groups on the surface of the two QDs. Both the IR absorption bands of the two QDs (Fig. S1a and S1b, see Supplementary Information) at around 1550–1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the COO− vibration, which represent the carboxyl groups conferring good water-solubility, and suggest that TGA and NAC have been successfully modified onto the gQDs and rQDs, respectively [39,40]. The peaks at 2993 cm−1 and 2929 cm−1 are due to CH stretching vibrations (Fig. S1b), corresponding to the ethanoyl groups of NAC [40].

The absorption spectra of the QDs in DI water were also measured (Fig. 2c). The absorption peak of the TGA-capped gQDs and NAC-capped rQDs were at 475 nm and 575 nm, respectively. The QDs can be excited in a broad spectra range from 300 nm to 500 nm with maximum at 315 nm for rQDs and 360 nm for gQDs (Fig. S2). To be compatible with commercial available fiber coupler, we chose laser at 405 nm to excite the QDs. Fig. 2d shows the emitted fluorescence spectra of the QDs excited at 405 nm and the maximal excitation wavelength. It can be seen that the laser at 405 nm is applicable for simultaneous excitation of both the rQDs and gQDs. The aqueous dispersed rQDs and gQDs under laser excitation exhibit bright green and red fluorescence, which could be easily observed with the naked eye (inset of Fig. 2d). The emission peaks of the rQDs and gQDs locate at 512 nm and 610 nm, respectively.

The QDs could be doped into the hydrogel matrices through solution mixing prior to photo-crosslinking. We chose the widely used biocompatible PEGDA hydrogels for immobilization of the QDs. Absorption spectroscopy was performed to show the optical loss of the QDs-doped hydrogel (Fig. 2e). The PEGDA hydrogels and QDs-PEGDA nanocomposite hydrogels were prepared in standard 1.5 mL (1 × 0.5 cm-wide) cuvettes (inset of Fig. 2e). The QDs-PEGDA hydrogels exhibited much higher optical loss than the PEGDA hydrogels, attributed to the absorption of the embedded QDs. No wavelength shift in absorption peaks of the QDs was observed after incorporated into the hydrogel network. Fig. 2f shows the emission spectra of QDs in polymerized hydrogels when excited at 405 nm. The peak emission wavelength of the gQDs and rQDs was observed at 520 nm and 628 nm, slightly redshifted in comparison with the QDs in DI water. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission spectra was 40 nm for gQDs and 55 nm for rQDs. The emission bands were well-resolved that facilitated the ratiometric measurements (Fig. 2f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber with step-index profile
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogel optical fiber doped with QDs was fabricated with a core/cladding step-index structure. For a step-index fiber, light is guided via total internal reflection at the interface between the core and the cladding layer, where the refractive of core should be larger than that of the cladding. The core was made by injecting the QDs-PEGDA precursors into a tube mold through a syringe and polymerized by UV exposure (Fig. 1a). To avoid mutual interferences, the two types of QDs were incorporated in discrete section of the hydrogel fiber. The refractive index (RI) of the core was measured to be ∼1.402(n
core ) by using a refractometer (ATAGO, PAL-RI). We used alginate hydrogel (1% w/v) for the cladding layer, which has a low refractive index of ∼1.333(n
cladding ). Then, the relative refractive index difference
( Δ =
n
c o r e
2
−
n
c l a d d i n g
2
2
n
c o r e
2
)
and numerical aperture
( N A =
n
c o r e
2
−
n
c l a d d i n g
2
)
of the step-index fiber would be 0.048 and 0.434, respectively. The cladding layer was formed on the core by using a dip-coating method (Fig. 1b). The thickness of the cladding layer can be controlled by varying the dipping time. Fig. 3 a shows the fabricated optical fibers at core/cladding diameters of 1.1/1.17 mm and 2.06/2.24 mm. Excitation laser at 405 nm was coupled to the hydrogel fiber through a pigtailed silica MMF, for which a short section of the silica fiber end was embedded in the core prior to photo-crosslinking (Fig. 3b). Strong red and green fluorescence were observed at the rQDs and gQDs doped regions of the hydrogel fiber. The pigtailed MMF has a NA of 0.2, corresponding to an acceptance angle of ∼11.5°. To suppress back reflection, the embedded silica fiber end was cleaved at 12° (Fig. 3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and pH dependence of the QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: We first studied the quenching effect of the QDs-doped hydrogel optical fiber (2.06/2.24 mm) to various metal ions. Fluorescence emission from the hydrogel fiber was measured through the pigtailed silica fiber using the setup in Fig. 1c. The hydrogel fiber was immersed in aqueous solutions, containing Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, As2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Na+ and Fe3+ ions, separately. The concentration of each metal ion was kept at 2.5 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence of the doping rQDs was significantly quenched by Fe3+ ions, while negligible changes in fluorescence intensity were observed in the presence of other metal ions, demonstrating high selectivity toward Fe3+ ions. As for the gQDs, the measured fluorescence kept stable under any of the tested metal ions, which therefore could serve as a stable and reliable reference to provide a built-in correction [24] (Fig. 4a). To explore the quenching mechanism, we investigated the UV–vis absorption spectra of QDs solution in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. S3). Upon addition of Fe3+ ions, no change occurred in the absorption spectra of the gQDs (Fig. S3a), while the rQDs exhibited slightly red-shift and decreased absorption peak, implying changes in size or surface properties of the rQDs (Fig. S3b). Since both QDs contain carboxyl groups, the quenching of the NAC-capped rQDs towards Fe3+ may attribute to the facilitation of non-radiative electron transfer process between the ethanoyl groups and Fe3+ ions (Fig. S1) [41].

We also examined the quenching effect of the QDs-doped hydrogel fiber in the presence of mixtures of all the metal ions above. The rQDs in the hydrogel fiber showed effective fluorescence quenching once adding Fe3+ ions to the metal ions mixtures, while the other coexisting interference ions showed negligible influences on the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4b). This result indicates that a stronger coordination interaction possibly occurred between the Fe3+ ions and the ethanoyl group of the capping molecule on surface of the rQDs than the other metal ions. In contrast, for the gQDs-doped section, the measured fluorescence kept stable in all cases.

The pH dependence of the QDs-doped hydrogel fiber was further investigated as shown in Fig. 4c. The fiber was dipped in different pH solutions in pH range of 1.68-12.5. The fluorescence intensities of the two types of QDs in hydrogel fiber were found highly dependent on pH. When immersed in strong acid solution at pH 1.68, both QDs obviously aggregated, which was confirmed by the TEM micrographs (Fig. S4). The aggregated QDs exhibited red-shift fluorescence spectra and significant fluorescence quenching (Fig. S5a and S5b). Moreover, the absorption of the QDs was found decreased and the absorption peaks disappeared (Fig. S5c and S5d). The acidic pH might induce the changes of the surface states of the QDs that led to the particle growth and agglomeration [42]. In the case of high pH (pH = 12.5), a small amount of aggregation appeared in gQDs, whereas the rQDs kept homo-disperse (Fig. S6). The gQDs in hydrogel fiber showed sharply decreased fluorescence intensity accompanied by slight decrease of absorption, while the rQDs were only slightly affected (Figs. 4c and S5). We deduced that the gQDs might be partly oxidized by ligand desorption, which quenched the fluorescence [42]. Considering the influence of pH on the QDs fluorescence, we chose PBS (pH = 7.4) for stable Fe3+ ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
We applied the QDs-doped hydrogel fiber for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions. The fiber was dipped in solutions of Fe3+ ions with gradually increased concentrations from 0 to 3.5 μM (Fig. 5 a). The measured emission spectra exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 520 nm and 628 nm, induced by the gQDs and rQDs, respectively. As expected, the emission intensity at 628 nm decreased with the increasing Fe3+ concentration, whereas the emission intensity at 520 nm kept almost unchanged. The ratio of the emission intensities at 628 nm and 520 nm (F = I 628/I 520) would provide a built-in correction for environmental effects. Fig. 5b shows a linear curve fitting via the following equation:
(2) (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.2707C + 0.0065 (R2 = 0.9877)

where F 0 is the intensity ratio of the hydrogel fiber in absence of Fe3+ ions and C denotes the concentration of Fe3+. The sensor exhibited a linear range of 0–3.5 μM and the detection limit was calculated to be 14 nM (S/N = 3). Time response of the hydrogel fiber to Fe3+ ions was further studied (Figs. 5c and S7). The emission intensity at 628 nm decayed rapidly once immersed in solutions of Fe3+ ions and saturated around 4 min later. In contrast, negligible changes were observed for the emission intensity at 520 nm over time. The fluorescence changes could be differentiated by naked eye (inset of Fig. 5c). The time-dependency of the fluorescence quenching was mainly attributed to molecular diffusion of the Fe3+ into the hydrogel matrix. Faster response of the hydrogel fiber to Fe3+ ions could be achieved for a higher Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5d) or thinner fiber diameter (Fig. 5e).

To evaluate the capability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting Fe3+ ions in real water sample, we spiked various concentrations of Fe3+ ions with tap water samples (water supply system of Tsinghua University) (Fig. S8). As the concentrations of the Fe3+ ions gradually increased, the sensor exhibited decreased fluorescence intensity and indicated a linear response (R2 = 0.9866) with the Fe3+ concentration over the range from 0 to 3.5 μM (Fig. 5f).

In summary, we have demonstrated a ratiometric fluorescence fiber sensor for selective and quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions based on QDs-doped hydrogel fiber. The fiber with core-cladding structure has been made and the relative refractive index difference between the core and the cladding layer is 0.048. The synthesized green emissive TGA capped QDs (gQDs) and red emissive NAC capped QDs (rQDs) were excited under a single wavelength and exhibited two well-resolved emission bands with peak at 520 nm and 628 nm, respectively. While the gQDs kept stable emission fluorescence in presence of all the tested metal ions, the rQDs showed selective quenching effect towards Fe3+ ions. The two types of QDs were doped in discrete sections of a step-index hydrogel optical fiber and ratiometric detection of the Fe3+ was achieved by the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at two different emission wavelengths. The hydrogel optical fiber served as a light-guiding platform for efficient laser delivery and emission collection in aqueous environments, where Fe3+ ions can penetrate into the hydrogel matrix via passive diffusion. Quantitative characterization of the ratiometric sensor for Fe3+ sensing indicated a linear range from 0 to 3.5 μM with a detection limit of 14 nM. Based on these results, the proposed ratiometric sensor is expected to provide a facile, portable and robust sensing platform for rapid on-site detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.119.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

pH and concentration dependence of the optical properties of thiol-capped CdTe nanocrystals in water and D2O
Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Minjuan Zhou received her Bachelor and Master degree from Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 2005, and 2007. She is currently a Ph.D. student in State Key Laboratory of Precision Measurement Technology and Instruments, Department of Precision Instruments, Tsinghua University. Her research interest focuses on optical fiber sensors base on fluorescence nanomaterials, surface enhanced Raman scattering technology, and their applications in chemical and biological detection.

Jingjing Guo received the B.S. degree in Optical information and science from Wuhan University of Technology in 2013. He is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree in fiber-optic sensors at Tsinghua University, China, under the supervision of Prof. C. Yang.

Changxi Yang received the Bachelor and Master degree from Nankai University in 1986 and in 1989, respectively. In 1992, he was granted the Ph.D. degree in non-linear fiber optics by the Institute of Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He joined the department of Precision Instruments, Tsinghua University as a professor, in 2001. He has long-standing interests in non-linear fiber optics, fiber lasers and fiber-optic sensors.
